subject: [PATCH v2 2/2] mm/vmpressure: split v1 userspace eventfd code into vmpressure-v1.c

reviews.author: Michal Koutný
reviews.summary: Reviewer Michal Koutný questioned the statement that tree=false cannot be removed, pointing out that the other patch already bails out from vmpressure() and nothing is updated anyway.
reviews.raw_body:
(Here I understand PSI is a different and differntly scope metric) but
what does it mean when you write that tree=false cannot be removed but
the other patch bails out from vmpressure() (i.e. nothing is updated
anyway)?

Thanks,
Michal

reviews.author: Usama Arif (author)
reviews.summary: Author clarified that the first patch only separates cgroup v2 from tree=true, leaving tree=false for networking socket-buffer back-off, and acknowledged this is a separate scope of work to switch to PSI.
reviews.raw_body:
So the first patch bails out for cgroup v2 for tree = true only.
For tree = false, it doesn't bail out, and is still used for networking
socket-buffer back-off. I think that is a whole another scope of work
switching to PSI. Hope that makes sense?

Thanks,
Usama

reviews.author: Michal Koutný
reviews.summary: Reviewer Michal Koutný noted that splitting off v1 code in patch 2/2 would have an unintended consequence, where tree=true code would not be compiled and its behavior would change due to the reordering of patches.
reviews.raw_body:
I've mixed mutliple things together, sorry. I wanted to actually ask
about your response:

| I realized when trying to swap the order that the splitting off v1
| commit will end up doing more that what I think it should do (just
| splitting off v1 specific code), as the tree = true code will not get
| compiled in at all for cgroup v2, and it then ends up changing more
| behaviour.

tree=true won't get compiled but v2 doesn't care about it, so the effect
of patch 2/2 should still be same (regardless whether it comes 1st or
2nd).
Do you refer to the invocation of vmpressure_v1_account_tree() that is
affected by this?

Thanks,
Michal

reviews.author: Usama Arif (author)
reviews.summary: Author clarified that they want to keep the optimization of not running vmpressure for tree=true + cgroup v2 as a separate patch, not included in the initial split of v1 code.
reviews.raw_body:
So what I mean is that I want to keep the effect of the patch that splits
off v1 code as just that and not have the optimization of not running
vmpressure for tree = true + cgroup v2.

vmpressure_v1_account_tree() compiles to an empty function in the split
patch for cgroup v2, so if we make the splitting out v1 code as the first
patch, the commit is not just going to split v1 code but also do the
optimization of not running tree = true cgroup v2.

I hope it makes sense?

reviews.author: Johannes Weiner
reviews.summary: Reviewer suggested moving the vmpressure eventfd interface into existing memcontrol-v1.c, rather than creating a new file for it.
reviews.raw_body:
Might as well move the interface part to memcontrol-v1.c instead of
creating a new file.

Otherwise looks good to me.

reviews.author: Michal Koutný
reviews.summary: Reviewer Michal Koutný expressed understanding of the patch after initial confusion, indicating that they have a clear grasp of the optimization and cleanup efforts.
sentiment_signals: reviewer's tone suggests clarity and understanding
reviews.raw_body:
All clear now, it's clicked for me what is the optimization and what the
cleanup.

Thanks,
Michal

reviews.author: Usama Arif (author)
reviews.summary: Author addressed Johannes Weiner's concern about the naming of vmpressure-v1.c, suggesting that it would be easier to rename it once cgroup v2 is moved off of vmpressure and gate it to CONFIG_MEMCG_V1. Alternatively, they propose keeping it separate in a file named after its feature.
reviews.raw_body:
I think it would make it easier once we move cgroup v2 off of
vmpressure. Then we can rename vmpressure-v1.c to just vmpressure.c
and gate it to CONFIG_MEMCG_V1. The other option would be everything
living in memcontrol-v1.c? I think its nice to keep it separate as
memcontrol-v1.c is already 2K+ lines and this is standalone feature
that can sit in a separate file.

reviews.author: Johannes Weiner
reviews.summary:
Reviewer suggested moving the code from mm/vmpressure-v1.c to memcontrol-v1.c, essentially swapping the destination of the split

Reviewer Johannes Weiner suggested moving the deprecated cgroup v1 userspace eventfd code into a contiguous block under memcg1_oom_finish(), prioritizing containing and making it easy to see the footprint of the cgroup1 option over maintaining neat component separation.
sentiment_signals: no clear technical objection or suggestion
reviews.raw_body:
Hm? I just mean move whatever you move into that new vmpressure-v1.c
into memcontrol-v1.c instead.

---

It's all deprecated code with no active development. Containing it and
making it easy to see the footprint of the cgroup1 option is the
higher priority, more than maintaining neat component separation.

No need to work it naturally into the file either - decls/variables up
top, functions somewhere further down etc. Just paste it into one
contiguous vmpressure block under memcg1_oom_finish(). That's how that
file is already structured with the other components in there.

2026-06-29.patch_summary: This patch splits the v1 userspace eventfd code from mm/vmpressure.c into a new file, mm/vmpressure-v1.c. The split is done to clean up the original file and prepare for eventually making vmpressure CONFIG_MEMCG_V1 only. The patch moves almost half of the code in mm/vmpressure.c into the new file, which is built only when CONFIG_MEMCG_V1 is enabled. This results in a significant reduction in memory usage on systems where CONFIG_MEMCG_V1 is not enabled. The medium-term plan is to introduce a PSI-based socket-pressure path and eventually remove the tree=false path entirely.
